Article content: {"plain_text":"Most people are using Claude Opus 4.8 to answer questions.
Save this :)
A small group of people have it running businesses while they sleep.
The difference is not the model. You both have access to the same Opus 4.8. The difference is that one group treats it like a search engine and the other treats it like a workforce, wiring it into systems that research, draft, organize, and produce real output on a schedule, with nobody sitting at the keyboard.
Let me be straight with you before we start, because the people selling you dreams won't be. "Makes money while you sleep" does not mean money appears from nothing. It means the labor runs while you sleep. The system does the work. You still need something to sell and someone to sell it to. And anything that touches real money or messages real people stays behind your approval, every single time. That's not a limitation. That's exactly what separates a real business from a scam.
With that understood, here are 40 workflows you can build on Opus 4.8 that do the work for you on repeat. I've grouped them so you can find the ones that fit what you already do. You don't need all 40. You need one, built properly.
Content and Audience Workflows
These are the highest-leverage workflows for anyone who already publishes anything, because they multiply work you're already doing.
1. The Content Repurposing Engine. You write or record one long-form piece. A scheduled workflow turns it into a thread, a set of standalone posts, a newsletter section, and a short script, each rewritten for its platform rather than copy-pasted. One input, a week of content, produced overnight. If you create anything at all, build this one first.
2. The Niche Newsletter on Autopilot. Pick a narrow topic you understand. A research agent gathers the week's developments, verifies them, and drafts the issue in your voice. You read it over coffee, edit, and send. The ninety percent that used to eat your week now happens while you sleep, and a paid newsletter in a real niche is a genuine recurring business.
3. The SEO Content System. A keyword goes in, a researched and structured draft comes out, fact-checked by a critic agent against its own sources. Run it on a schedule and you wake up to a backlog of drafts. The honest part: you still review, and you still need a real site people visit. The model writes; it doesn't market for you.
4. The Lead Magnet Factory. Turn your expertise into checklists, templates, guides, and mini-courses. A workflow drafts each one from a topic and an outline. You polish and publish. A steady stream of free, genuinely useful lead magnets is the top of every real funnel, and now you produce them in batches instead of one painful afternoon at a time.
5. The Thread and Carousel Builder. Feed it any article and it produces a tight thread and a carousel outline, structured for the platform, with hooks at the front. The people who post consistently win, and consistency is a systems problem. This workflow solves it.
6. The Video-to-Everything Pipeline. Drop in a transcript from a video or podcast and the workflow produces show notes, timestamped highlights, a written article, and a set of pull-quotes. One recording becomes a week of assets across formats. Creators leave enormous value on the table by publishing once and stopping.
7. The Content Calendar Engine. A workflow plans a month of content around your themes, drafts the first version of each piece, and slots them into a schedule. You start every week with a full pipeline instead of a blank page and a knot in your stomach.
8. The Engagement Draft Assistant. It reads the comments and replies you need to respond to, drafts thoughtful responses in your voice, and queues them for your approval. Engagement is how audiences grow, and it's tedious enough that most people quit. This makes it sustainable. You approve and post; the drafting is done.
Service-Business Workflows
These are workflows where you sell an outcome to a client and the system does the heavy lifting. This is how one person runs an agency.
9. The Data Cleaning Service. Messy spreadsheets are everywhere and businesses pay to fix them. With Claude in Excel and the code execution tool, you build a workflow that takes a malformed file and returns a clean, structured, documented one. You sell the result; the system does the cleanup.
10. The Document-to-Deliverable Pipeline. A client sends raw notes, a transcript, or a rough brief. Your workflow returns a finished Word document, a polished deck, or a formatted PDF. File creation means the output is the actual deliverable, not text to reformat. This is a real, sellable service you can run from anywhere.
11. The Resume and Profile Optimizer. Someone sends their resume and a target role; your workflow returns a rewritten version and a profile rewrite tuned to that role. Productize it at a fixed price. The work is repeatable, which means it's automatable, which means it scales past your own hours.
12. The Social Management Workflow. Run content scheduling, drafting, and reply suggestions for a client as a service. Drafts generate on a schedule; you and the client approve what actually posts. One person can credibly run this for several clients once the system is built.
13. The Support Knowledge Agent. Feed a Project your product docs and FAQs and build an agent that drafts accurate answers to common questions. Offer it to a small business drowning in repetitive support. The agent drafts; a human confirms anything consequential before it goes out.
14. The Inbound Qualifier. A workflow reads incoming inquiries, sorts them by fit and urgency, drafts a tailored first reply, and surfaces the ones worth your time. It reads and drafts overnight; you approve and send the replies that matter in the morning. Sending always stays with you.
15. The Inbox Triage Service. For a busy professional, a workflow that reads, categorizes, and summarizes the day's email and drafts replies to the routine ones is worth real money. You're selling someone their mornings back. Nothing sends without a human pressing the button.
16. The Meeting-to-Action Pipeline. Drop in a meeting transcript and get a clean summary, a list of decisions, and a list of action items with owners. Offer it as a service or run it for your own team. It turns the thing everyone forgets to do into something that just happens.
17. The Localization Service. A workflow that adapts content into another language and register, not a flat translation but a genuine rewrite that lands in the target culture. If you understand two markets, this is a service businesses pay well for, because most translation reads like translation.
18. The Editing and Proofing Service. A workflow that cleans up grammar, tightens prose, and flags unclear passages, returning a marked-up and a clean version. Writers, students, and businesses all need this. You're selling polish, and the system delivers the first pass.
Asset-Building Workflows
These build something you own, that keeps earning after the work is done. This is the closest thing on the list to true passive income.
19. Build and Sell Plugins. Package your best workflow into a plugin and list it in the marketplace. You build the asset once and it sells while you sleep. This is genuine passive income, because the product is the system.
20. Build and Sell Artifacts. Claude can build interactive tools: calculators, trackers, generators, mini-apps, now with persistent storage so they remember user data between visits. Build a genuinely useful one, share it, and let it become a product or a lead magnet that works around the clock.
21. The Digital Product Engine. Turn your knowledge into a structured course, a workbook, or a template pack. A workflow drafts each module from your outline. You own the product, sell it on repeat, and the heavy lifting of the first draft happened overnight.
22. The Micro-SaaS Prototype. Use Claude Code to go from idea to a working product with payments and analytics, even if you're not a deep developer. The build is faster than ever and the asset is yours. The caveat the hype skips: shipping is the easy twenty percent, and supporting real users is the other eighty. But the door to owning software is wider open than it has ever been.
23. The Prompt and Template Pack. Build a focused, genuinely useful pack of prompts or templates for a specific profession and sell it. A workflow helps you generate, test, and document each one. Niche and excellent beats broad and generic every time.
24. The Notion and System Template Business. If you build good systems, package them as templates other people install. A workflow helps you document, write the setup guide, and produce the marketing copy. People pay to skip the work of designing a system from scratch.
25. The Niche Directory Site. A workflow researches, structures, and writes entries for a directory in a category you know well, kept fresh on a schedule. A useful directory earns through ads, listings, or affiliate links, and the content engine runs without you. The integrity rule: the entries have to be accurate, or the directory dies.
26. The Browser Tool. Build a small browser extension or web tool that solves one annoying problem for a specific audience, using Claude Code to build it and Claude to write the listing. A tool that does one thing well can earn quietly for years.
Sales and Outreach Workflows
These prepare the work of selling. The hard rule throughout: the system prepares, you send.
27. The Outreach Personalization Engine. A workflow researches each prospect and drafts a genuinely personalized first message, not a mail-merge with a name slotted in. It does the research and drafting overnight; you review and send the ones worth sending. Personalization at scale is the holy grail of outreach, and this is as close as it gets, with a human firmly on the send button.
28. The Lead Research and Enrichment Workflow. Give it a list of names or companies and it gathers public, relevant context on each, so your outreach actually knows who it's talking to. Tedious research becomes an overnight task. Where you contact those leads, you do it; the workflow just makes you informed.
29. The Proposal and Quote Generator. A workflow that turns a short brief into a structured, professional proposal or quote in your format. It collapses the hours you spend writing proposals into minutes of review. You still set the price and stand behind the offer.
30. The Follow-Up Sequence Drafter. It drafts thoughtful, non-spammy follow-up messages tuned to where each conversation stands. Most deals die from lack of follow-up, not lack of interest. The drafts are ready; you decide what actually goes out.
31. The CRM Cleanup Workflow. A workflow that reads a messy contact list, standardizes it, fills gaps with public information, and flags duplicates. Clean data makes every other sales workflow work better, and this is the unglamorous step everyone avoids.
Research and Intelligence Workflows
These don't sell something directly. They make you the best-informed operator in your space, which over time is worth more than any single product.
32. The Daily Competitive Brief. A scheduled task and a Live Artifact pull from your connectors every morning and assemble a dashboard of what your competitors and market did overnight. You don't sell the brief, but you make sharper decisions before your competitors have had coffee. That edge compounds.
33. The Opportunity Scanner. Point a research workflow at the spaces you care about and have it surface gaps, unmet needs, and emerging trends on a schedule. The best businesses come from spotting an opening early. This makes spotting openings a daily habit instead of a lucky accident.
34. The Market Report Product. Build a workflow that produces a specific, valuable research report on demand: a market scan, a competitor teardown, a niche trend brief. Sell it as a fixed-price product. The system does the analysis; you do the selling. This is how one person runs a research shop.
35. The Trend Monitor. A workflow that watches your chosen sources and alerts you when something genuinely relevant happens, with a short summary of why it matters. You stop drowning in feeds and start getting only what counts, assembled while you sleep.
36. The Review-Mining Workflow. Point it at public reviews and feedback in your market and have it extract the patterns: what people love, what they hate, what they keep asking for. This is product gold, and almost nobody does the work to gather it systematically. The system does it overnight.
Operations and Back-Office Workflows
These run the unglamorous machinery of a business so you don't have to. They don't make money directly; they save the hours that let you make money.
37. The Bookkeeping Draft Assistant. A workflow that reads your transaction exports, categorizes them, flags anomalies, and drafts a monthly summary. It does not file your taxes and it does not move your money, both of which stay firmly with you and your accountant. But it turns hours of reconciliation into a review you do over breakfast.
38. The Personal Operations Brief. Every morning, a scheduled task assembles your calendar, your tasks, your inbox highlights, and your top three priorities into one brief. You don't sell it. It just makes you the kind of operator who starts every day already oriented, which over a year is worth more than most products on this list.
39. The Invoice and Contract Drafter. A workflow that drafts invoices, standard contracts, and statements of work from a short brief in your templates. The routine paperwork that eats your afternoons gets prepared automatically. You review the numbers and the terms, then send.
40. The SOP Generator. A workflow that turns "how I do this task" into a clean, documented standard operating procedure. This is the secret weapon: every SOP you generate is a task you can eventually hand to a workflow or a person. It's the workflow that builds all your other workflows.
How to Actually Build One This Week
Forty workflows is a menu, not a to-do list. Trying to build all of them is how you finish none of them.
Pick one. Pick the one closest to something you already do or already sell, because a workflow that automates work you understand will always beat a workflow for a business you're only imagining.
Then build it in this order. First, do the task manually with Claude a few times until you understand every step. Second, write the process down, because a workflow is just a process written clearly enough that Claude can follow it. Third, build it as a Project with clear instructions and the connectors it needs. Fourth, add a critic step so the output is good without you checking every line. Fifth, and only once it works reliably by hand, put it on a schedule with Cowork so it runs without you.
The temptation is to skip straight to step five and automate something that doesn't work yet. Resist it. Automating a broken process just produces broken output faster, on a schedule, while you sleep, which is the worst version of all.
The Two Rules That Keep This Real
Anything irreversible stays behind your approval. Sending messages, moving money, posting publicly, deleting things. The system can draft and prepare all of it. You press the button. Always. This isn't caution for its own sake; it's the difference between a tool that serves you and one that embarrasses you while you sleep.
The output is your responsibility. When you put your name on a report, a review, a reply, or a proposal, you own whether it's true and good. The model drafts. You are accountable. The people who build durable income with these workflows treat the model's output as a sharp first draft from a fast junior, not as finished gospel.
These two rules are not the boring fine print. They're the reason your business survives past month three.
The Real Money Is in Stacking Them
Here's what separates the people making a little from the people making a lot.
One workflow is useful. Stacked workflows are a business. The operators who pull serious income from this aren't running one clever automation. They're running a chain where the output of one becomes the input of the next, and the whole thing compounds.
Picture it. The opportunity scanner (33) surfaces a gap in your market. The market report product (34) turns that gap into something you can sell. The content repurposing engine (1) turns the report's findings into a week of posts that attract the exact buyers who need it. The lead magnet factory (4) hands those readers something useful in exchange for their email. The outreach personalization engine (27) drafts a tailored note to the warmest of them, which you review and send. The proposal generator (29) turns an interested reply into a clean offer. And the SOP generator (40) documents the whole chain so you can hand pieces of it off or rebuild it for the next niche.
That's not seven separate side projects. That's one machine, where you're the only human in the loop at the moments that actually require judgment. Each individual workflow is modest. The chain is a company that mostly runs itself.
You don't build the chain on day one. You build one link, prove it works, then add the next link it naturally feeds into. But knowing the chain is the goal changes which workflow you pick first, because you start choosing links that connect instead of isolated tricks.
The Honest Truth About Making Money in Your Sleep
There is no workflow that creates value out of nothing.
Every one of these makes money the same way work has always made money: by producing something people want and getting it in front of them. What changed is that the producing part, the part that used to eat your nights and weekends, can now run without you. That is an enormous shift. It is not a magic money printer, and anyone who tells you otherwise is selling you the dream, not the system.
The people who win with this are not the ones hunting for the laziest possible setup. They're the ones who use the time the automation gives back to do the things that still need a human: build relationships, make the judgment calls, find the next opportunity, and close the sale. The automation handles the labor so you can spend your hours on the things only you can do.
Most people will read all forty of these, feel a jolt of excitement, and build nothing.
A small group will pick one this week, build it properly, and let it start doing the work that used to keep them up at night.
Six months of that and you're not working harder. You're running systems.
The model is ready. You have the menu. The only question left is which one you build first, today.
If you found this useful, follow me @eng_khairallah1 for more AI content like this. I post breakdowns, courses, and tools every week.
